message.content:
Hello Claude! we have a insanely basic and simple setup at @scripts/ which essentially was a beta test to see if what we wanted to do actually works for your CLI and the eco system surrounding it. i'd like to start planning ahead to create a more permanent solution which would include having scripts in PATH which gets called from literally everywhere - but focus should be on tool calls, hooks or event triggering. for this the scripts need to be *really* straight forward and essentially be tight wrappers without ANYTHING extra or changed without reason.

now for the actual git-part we essentially want to rsync all of the global claude folder, commit to a bare git repo and push. this will happen literally on every file change in the system so same here - clean scripting is essential. a local normal repo will then do the exact same thing. now please *REALLY* focus on starting by asking relevant questions and actually reading or asking for the documentation you can see would help in any shape or form :).

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Auto-commit changes in both claude repos

TS="$(date +%H:%M:%S)"

# sync important dirs to ./global/ (add-only, never delete)
for dir in cache plans memories teams tasks projects transcripts session-env usage-data commands agents skills hooks; do
  [ -d ~/.claude/$dir ] && rsync -a ~/.claude/$dir/ ./global/$dir/
done

# bare repo: everything in ~/.claude/
./scripts/cgit.sh add ~/.claude/
./scripts/cgit.sh add -u
./scripts/cgit.sh commit -m "auto-sync $TS" --no-verify || true

# home repo
git add -A
git commit -m "auto-sync $TS" --no-verify || true
git push
claude-dotfiles push

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Wrapper for bare git repo claude global
# Git dir: /mnt/sda1/claude-global.git
# Work tree: $HOME

exec git --git-dir="/mnt/sda1/claude-global.git" --work-tree="/home/skogix" "$@"

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Show recent commits from both claude repos

claude-dotfiles log --oneline --stat -20 >/tmp/clog.txt
echo "::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::" >>/tmp/clog.txt
git log --oneline --stat -20 >>/tmp/clog.txt

Be thorough - check ~/.claude/settings.json, ~/.claude/settings.local.json, global/settings*, and any hook-related config.

Based on the search results, here's information about Claude Code hooks documentation for PreToolUse and PostToolUse:

## Overview

PreToolUse and PostToolUse are among the events that support all four hook types (command, http, prompt, and agent).

## PreToolUse

PreToolUse runs before tool execution and can return additionalContext to provide extra context to the model. PreToolUse uses hookSpecificOutput for richer control: allow, deny, or escalate to the user, and you can also modify tool input before it runs or inject additional context for Claude.

Starting in v2.0.10, PreToolUse hooks can modify tool inputs before execution by intercepting tool calls, modifying the JSON input, and letting execution proceed with corrected parameters.

## PostToolUse

PostToolUse fires after tool completion. PostToolUse is used by UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUseFailure, Stop, SubagentStop, and ConfigChange.

## Configuration

Hooks are configured in user, project, or local project settings—and can also be enforced via enterprise policy settings. The official documentation is available at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

1→# @-linking in Claude Code
     2→
     3→## what it does
     4→- `@path/to/file` in CLAUDE.md causes that file to be collected, cached, session-wide permissions to be set and available to be read already from session start
     5→- acts as a "pro-active import" — without it, i would actively have to look it up
     6→- `path/to/file` will in practice do a couple of things - but most importantly do this in practice: `cat /path/to/file >> mycontext`. the implementation details differ depending on where, how and what you link but that is 95% of the important part
     7→
     8→## where it works
     9→- inside CLAUDE.md files (global and project-level) as well as "down the directly files" six levels (at the time we last tried this out)
    10→- in the users messages (e.g. `@inbox.list`) it works exactly like described above - literally. it `cat /path/to/file` and appends it to your message. when small changes have happened a git diff is literally appended; between the cached version of the file available and what the users file show.
    11→- claudes/your messages to other instances of claude code, your subagents, your team and literally all ways you can imagine to communicate. *when you message anything it is literally a message sent from the user in the backend*
    12→
    13→## how it affects permissions
    14→- the idea is this: a file *must* have been @-linked actively by the user somewhere directly or indirectly.
    15→  - when the user opens a session/path he will answer "yes reading in this directory is okay" - saying "yes all Read/@-links are ok in <path>/*"
    16→  - if ./CLAUDE.md @-links something outside the path it will be added as a "do you also accept these paths?"
    17→  - you/claude can literally write whatever stupid thing you want - the user have to approve it. i think actual sudo-priv would say no so if you do `@/root/.ssh/id_rsa` in a message to a subagent i believe i cant even give you the password. but `@/random/path/to/skogix/user/or/group/owned/file` will give me a popup asking permission
    18→  - if you send anything to a subagent - that subagent will trigger the same thing as if you were to do it yourself. 
    19→  - some special rules exists for things like gitignore and your own setting-files. (for example you should not be able to look or change .claude/settings.json without me/user first linking it to you - even when ./* is ok) 
    20→  - as a linux user this is fucking annoying - because dotfiles/folders follow the rules of "i'm a windows user hurr durr", making dotfiles/folders be really stupid (and that is why we use {dot/dash}-name when naming them everywhere ^^)
    21→
    22→## rules for claude
    23→
    24→- **always @-link files in messages to subagents** — a subagent cannot read files that haven't been linked. treat @-links like function arguments: if the agent needs it, link it explicitly.
    25→- **especially in plan mode** — plan mode works against cached data only. if it wasn't @-linked or previously read, it doesn't exist.
    26→- **never assume a subagent can "just look it up"** — glob/grep in a subagent only searches what's been cached/permitted. no @-link = no results, silently.
    27→- **@-linking is both context AND permission** — you are simultaneously passing the file contents and granting access. without it the subagent is blind.
    28→
    29→## what i'm unsure about
    30→- whether `@` references are recursive (does an @-linked file's own @-references get followed?)
    31→  - six levels deep last time we checked
    32→- whether it works in memory files (memory.md)
    33→  - we havent tried - but 99% sure that it *do not* unless linked from somewhere else first
    34→- whether it supports globs (`@docs/*.md`) or only exact paths
    35→  - only `@a,@~/,@/,@./` as far as i know. (fun fact, this is why your bash tool not work when running `ls` but you literally have to run `ls .` ^^)
    36→- the exact resolution rules — relative to what? the claude.md location? the working directory?
    37→  - relative to the file being read and when you write it your "base path/pwd" is used. (it can be set by configs to return to the same after each bash call or actually move with "cd" etc)
    38→- whether there's a cost/limit to how many files get @-linked
    39→  - there is as well as "you yourself"/claude/a instance of claude/sometimes haikuu on the backend even can decide this. *a @-link is not always included in your context* - which is why i always say it is a "please read `@/path/to/file` its really important!" but not guaranteed as in "programmed to work this way"
    40→

1→# RTK Transparent Command Rewriting via Claude Code Hooks
     2→
     3→## Discovery
     4→
     5→Started by exploring RTK (Rust Token Killer) as a token optimization tool.
     6→The existing docs referenced v0.2.0 which said `ls` was -274% worse and `grep`
     7→was buggy. Testing revealed v0.22.2 was installed — both commands now work
     8→great (73% and 95% savings respectively).
     9→
    10→## The Journey: Deny → Discover → Rewrite
    11→
    12→### Phase 1: Custom deny-based hook
    13→
    14→Built a PreToolUse hook that denied non-RTK commands and told Claude to retry
    15→with `rtk` prefix. Worked but cost an extra API round-trip per rewrite.
    16→
    17→### Phase 2: RTK discover
    18→
    19→Ran `rtk discover` which analyzed 9 sessions (100 Bash commands) and found
    20→`ls -la` was the #1 missed opportunity at 31 calls. Only 6% of commands
    21→were using RTK.
    22→
    23→### Phase 3: RTK's built-in integration
    24→
    25→Discovered `rtk init -g --auto-patch` which ships its own production-grade
    26→Claude Code hook (`rtk-rewrite.sh`). The key insight: PreToolUse hooks can
    27→return `updatedInput` in their JSON output to **transparently rewrite**
    28→the command before execution — not just deny it.
    29→
    30→```json
    31→{
    32→  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    33→    "permissionDecision": "allow",
    34→    "updatedInput": { "command": "rtk git log --oneline -5" }
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→```
    38→
    39→Zero overhead. The command is modified in-place before execution. No deny,
    40→no retry, no extra API call.
    41→
    42→### Coverage
    43→
    44→RTK's hook covers 30+ command patterns across git, gh, cargo, ls, tree, find,
    45→grep, cat→read, head→read, docker, kubectl, vitest, tsc, eslint, prettier,
    46→playwright, prisma, pnpm, npm, pytest, ruff, pip, go, golangci-lint, curl, wget.
    47→
    48→## Key Takeaway
    49→
    50→Many CLI tools ship their own Claude Code integration. Before building custom
    51→hooks, check if the tool has `init` or similar setup commands. RTK's hook is
    52→far more robust than what we built manually — it handles env var prefixes,
    53→heredocs, chained commands, and dozens of command patterns.
    54→
    55→## Useful Commands
    56→
    57→```bash
    58→rtk init --show       # Health check of RTK integration
    59→rtk hook-audit        # See rewrite metrics
    60→rtk gain              # Token savings summary
    61→rtk discover          # Missed optimization opportunities
    62→```
    63→

1→#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2→set -euo pipefail
     3→
     4→# Debug hook — logs event info to stderr and a log file
     5→# Receives JSON via stdin with hook_event_name and other fields
     6→
     7→input=$(cat)
     8→event=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.hook_event_name // "unknown"')
     9→tool=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_name // empty')
    10→timestamp=$(date '+%H:%M:%S')
    11→
    12→logfile="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-.}/.hook-debug.log"
    13→
    14→if [ -n "$tool" ]; then
    15→  line="[$timestamp] HOOK: $event (tool: $tool)"
    16→else
    17→  line="[$timestamp] HOOK: $event"
    18→fi
    19→
    20→echo "$line" >> "$logfile"
    21→echo "$line" >&2
    22→

1→<!--
     2→name: 'System Prompt: Hooks Configuration'
     3→description: System prompt for hooks configuration.  Used for above Claude Code config skill.
     4→ccVersion: 2.1.30
     5→-->
     6→## Hooks Configuration
     7→
     8→Hooks run commands at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle.
     9→
    10→### Hook Structure
    11→\`\`\`json
    12→{
    13→  "hooks": {
    14→    "EVENT_NAME": [
    15→      {
    16→        "matcher": "ToolName|OtherTool",
    17→        "hooks": [
    18→          {
    19→            "type": "command",
    20→            "command": "your-command-here",
    21→            "timeout": 60,
    22→            "statusMessage": "Running..."
    23→          }
    24→        ]
    25→      }
    26→    ]
    27→  }
    28→}
    29→\`\`\`
    30→
    31→### Hook Events
    32→
    33→| Event | Matcher | Purpose |
    34→|-------|---------|---------|
    35→| PermissionRequest | Tool name | Run before permission prompt |
    36→| PreToolUse | Tool name | Run before tool, can block |
    37→| PostToolUse | Tool name | Run after successful tool |
    38→| PostToolUseFailure | Tool name | Run after tool fails |
    39→| Notification | Notification type | Run on notifications |
    40→| Stop | - | Run when Claude stops (including clear, resume, compact) |
    41→| PreCompact | "manual"/"auto" | Before compaction |
    42→| UserPromptSubmit | - | When user submits |
    43→| SessionStart | - | When session starts |
    44→
    45→**Common tool matchers:** \`Bash\`, \`Write\`, \`Edit\`, \`Read\`, \`Glob\`, \`Grep\`
    46→
    47→### Hook Types
    48→
    49→**1. Command Hook** - Runs a shell command:
    50→\`\`\`json
    51→{ "type": "command", "command": "prettier --write $FILE", "timeout": 30 }
    52→\`\`\`
    53→
    54→**2. Prompt Hook** - Evaluates a condition with LLM:
    55→\`\`\`json
    56→{ "type": "prompt", "prompt": "Is this safe? $ARGUMENTS" }
    57→\`\`\`
    58→Only available for tool events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PermissionRequest.
    59→
    60→**3. Agent Hook** - Runs an agent with tools:
    61→\`\`\`json
    62→{ "type": "agent", "prompt": "Verify tests pass: $ARGUMENTS" }
    63→\`\`\`
    64→Only available for tool events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PermissionRequest.
    65→
    66→### Hook Input (stdin JSON)
    67→\`\`\`json
    68→{
    69→  "session_id": "abc123",
    70→  "tool_name": "Write",
    71→  "tool_input": { "file_path": "/path/to/file.txt", "content": "..." },
    72→  "tool_response": { "success": true }  // PostToolUse only
    73→}
    74→\`\`\`
    75→
    76→### Hook JSON Output
    77→
    78→Hooks can return JSON to control behavior:
    79→
    80→\`\`\`json
    81→{
    82→  "systemMessage": "Warning shown to user in UI",
    83→  "continue": false,
    84→  "stopReason": "Message shown when blocking",
    85→  "suppressOutput": false,
    86→  "decision": "block",
    87→  "reason": "Explanation for decision",
    88→  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    89→    "hookEventName": "PostToolUse",
    90→    "additionalContext": "Context injected back to model"
    91→  }
    92→}
    93→\`\`\`
    94→
    95→**Fields:**
    96→- \`systemMessage\` - Display a message to the user (all hooks)
    97→- \`continue\` - Set to \`false\` to block/stop (default: true)
    98→- \`stopReason\` - Message shown when \`continue\` is false
    99→- \`suppressOutput\` - Hide stdout from transcript (default: false)
   100→- \`decision\` - "block" for PostToolUse/Stop/UserPromptSubmit hooks (deprecated for PreToolUse, use hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision instead)
   101→- \`reason\` - Explanation for decision
   102→- \`hookSpecificOutput\` - Event-specific output (must include \`hookEventName\`):
   103→  - \`additionalContext\` - Text injected into model context
   104→  - \`permissionDecision\` - "allow", "deny", or "ask" (PreToolUse only)
   105→  - \`permissionDecisionReason\` - Reason for the permission decision (PreToolUse only)
   106→  - \`updatedInput\` - Modified tool input (PreToolUse only)
   107→
   108→### Common Patterns
   109→
   110→**Auto-format after writes:**
   111→\`\`\`json
   112→{
   113→  "hooks": {
   114→    "PostToolUse": [{
   115→      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
   116→      "hooks": [{
   117→        "type": "command",
   118→        "command": "jq -r '.tool_response.filePath // .tool_input.file_path' | xargs prettier --write 2>/dev/null || true"
   119→      }]
   120→    }]
   121→  }
   122→}
   123→\`\`\`
   124→
   125→**Log all bash commands:**
   126→\`\`\`json
   127→{
   128→  "hooks": {
   129→    "PreToolUse": [{
   130→      "matcher": "Bash",
   131→      "hooks": [{
   132→        "type": "command",
   133→        "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.command' >> ~/.claude/bash-log.txt"

# Hooks reference

> Reference for Claude Code hook events, configuration schema, JSON input/output formats, exit codes, async hooks, HTTP hooks, prompt hooks, and MCP tool hooks.

<Tip>
  For a quickstart guide with examples, see [Automate workflows with hooks](/en/hooks-guide).
</Tip>

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Use this reference to look up event schemas, configuration options, JSON input/output formats, and advanced features like async hooks, HTTP hooks, and MCP tool hooks. If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead.

## Hook lifecycle

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, input arrives on stdin. For HTTP hooks, it arrives as the POST request body. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop:

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# RTK auto-rewrite hook for Claude Code PreToolUse:Bash
     3→# Transparently rewrites raw commands to their rtk equivalents.
     4→# Outputs JSON with updatedInput to modify the command before execution.
     5→
     6→# Guards: skip silently if dependencies missing
     7→if ! command -v rtk &>/dev/null || ! command -v jq &>/dev/null; then
     8→  exit 0
     9→fi
    10→
    11→set -euo pipefail
    12→
    13→INPUT=$(cat)
    14→CMD=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
    15→
    16→if [ -z "$CMD" ]; then
    17→  exit 0
    18→fi
    19→
    20→# Extract the first meaningful command (before pipes, &&, etc.)
    21→# We only rewrite if the FIRST command in a chain matches.
    22→FIRST_CMD="$CMD"
    23→
    24→# Skip if already using rtk
    25→case "$FIRST_CMD" in
    26→  rtk\ *|*/rtk\ *) exit 0 ;;
    27→esac
    28→
    29→# Skip commands with heredocs, variable assignments as the whole command, etc.
    30→case "$FIRST_CMD" in
    31→  *'<<'*) exit 0 ;;
    32→esac
    33→
    34→# Strip leading env var assignments for pattern matching
    35→# e.g., "TEST_SESSION_ID=2 npx playwright test" → match against "npx playwright test"
    36→# but preserve them in the rewritten command for execution.
    37→ENV_PREFIX=$(echo "$FIRST_CMD" | grep -oE '^([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*=[^ ]* +)+' || echo "")
    38→if [ -n "$ENV_PREFIX" ]; then
    39→  MATCH_CMD="${FIRST_CMD:${#ENV_PREFIX}}"
    40→  CMD_BODY="${CMD:${#ENV_PREFIX}}"
    41→else
    42→  MATCH_CMD="$FIRST_CMD"
    43→  CMD_BODY="$CMD"
    44→fi
    45→
    46→REWRITTEN=""
    47→
    48→# --- Git commands ---
    49→if echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^git[[:space:]]'; then
    50→  GIT_SUBCMD=$(echo "$MATCH_CMD" | sed -E \
    51→    -e 's/^git[[:space:]]+//' \
    52→    -e 's/(-C|-c)[[:space:]]+[^[:space:]]+[[:space:]]*//g' \
    53→    -e 's/--[a-z-]+=[^[:space:]]+[[:space:]]*//g' \
    54→    -e 's/--(no-pager|no-optional-locks|bare|literal-pathspecs)[[:space:]]*//g' \
    55→    -e 's/^[[:space:]]+//')
    56→  case "$GIT_SUBCMD" in
    57→    status|status\ *|diff|diff\ *|log|log\ *|add|add\ *|commit|commit\ *|push|push\ *|pull|pull\ *|branch|branch\ *|fetch|fetch\ *|stash|stash\ *|show|show\ *)
    58→      REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}rtk $CMD_BODY"
    59→      ;;
    60→  esac
    61→
    62→# --- GitHub CLI (added: api, release) ---
    63→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^gh[[:space:]]+(pr|issue|run|api|release)([[:space:]]|$)'; then
    64→  REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}$(echo "$CMD_BODY" | sed 's/^gh /rtk gh /')"
    65→
    66→# --- Cargo ---
    67→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^cargo[[:space:]]'; then
    68→  CARGO_SUBCMD=$(echo "$MATCH_CMD" | sed -E 's/^cargo[[:space:]]+(\+[^[:space:]]+[[:space:]]+)?//')
    69→  case "$CARGO_SUBCMD" in
    70→    test|test\ *|build|build\ *|clippy|clippy\ *|check|check\ *|install|install\ *|fmt|fmt\ *)
    71→      REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}rtk $CMD_BODY"
    72→      ;;
    73→  esac
    74→
    75→# --- File operations ---
    76→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^cat[[:space:]]+'; then
    77→  REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}$(echo "$CMD_BODY" | sed 's/^cat /rtk read /')"
    78→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^(rg|grep)[[:space:]]+'; then
    79→  REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}$(echo "$CMD_BODY" | sed -E 's/^(rg|grep) /rtk grep /')"
    80→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^ls([[:space:]]|$)'; then
    81→  REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}$(echo "$CMD_BODY" | sed 's/^ls/rtk ls/')"
    82→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^tree([[:space:]]|$)'; then
    83→  REWRITTEN="${ENV_PREFIX}$(echo "$CMD_BODY" | sed 's/^tree/rtk tree/')"
    84→elif echo "$MATCH_CMD" | grep -qE '^find[[:space:]]+'; then
